retrieved_docs.title: Nonalcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Different lipids have different potential to cause toxicity. Accumulation of liver triglycerides helps to buffer toxic lipids to lipid droplets enriched with inert triglycerides. Thus, hepatic triglyceride accumulation, which typically manifests as hepatic steatosis, is perhaps a defense mechanism to handle energy surplus in the liver. Toxic fat accumulation in the hepatocytes is caused by nontriglyceride metabolites of free fatty acids. Triglyceride in the lipid droplet form is a non-toxic mechanism to store fatty acids in the inert form. The lipotoxic metabolites of FFA have not been fully identified yet; these likely include saturated fatty acids, free cholesterol, ceramides, lysophosphatidylcholine, and diacylglycerol. The linearity of the two hit hypothesis is questionable as it has not always been shown that isolated steatosis precedes NASH, and the type of fat that typically accumulates in the liver with isolated steatosis (neutral fat or triglyceride) is different from the toxic fat (accumulation of which leads to NASH). The two-hit model does not explain why the first hit (steatosis) occurs. The presence of steatosis in itself might be a marker of hepatotoxic stress, and it is likely that NASH follows when the adaptive responses to the hepatocyte stress fail to maintain hepatocyte viability.

retrieved_docs.title: Cosmeceuticals -- Introduction -- DEFINITIONS & CONTROVERSIES
retrieved_docs.content: The most practical definition of this term may be - a cosmetic product that is purported to have therapeutic action capable of affecting the skin positively beyond the time of its application. Although the term cosmeceutical is steeped in dermatology literature and dominates academic discussions, symposia, and lectures around the world, it is strangely interesting that almost four decades after coining the term, this category of skincare products is still not formally recognized by the United States Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA) or the European Union. This disparity stems from the differentiation between 'cosmetics' and 'drugs' by the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) based on their intended use and ability to affect the structure and function of the cutis. Both in the United States and the European Union, a drug is defined as "an article intended for the use in the diagnosis, mitigation, treatment or prevention of disease or intended to affect the structure or any function of the body." [3] In contrast, the FD&C Act of 1938 defines cosmetics as "articles intended to be rubbed, poured, sprinkled, or sprayed on, introduced into, or otherwise applied to the human body or any part thereof for cleansing, beautifying, promoting attractiveness, or altering the appearance" without affecting structure or function. [3] In India, a well-defined Drugs and Cosmetics Act (1940) operates the regulations of cosmetics under the authority of the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO). But CDSCO also recognizes drugs and cosmetics, not cosmeceutical. In a nutshell, it is unfortunate that despite the ubiquitous presence, sale, marketing, and prescription by dermatologists, to date, there is no international consensus. These impact of this deficiency in regulatory nomenclature has far-reaching consequences concerning the essentials of product labeling [prescription or over the counter (OTC)], the stringency of testing protocol, and approval for sale and distribution. In the U.S., for example, in contrast to strict laws for drugs, there is no requirement for manufacturers to demonstrate either safety or efficacy before marketing a product that would otherwise qualify as a cosmeceutical, as is the case for drugs.  The Japanese authorities identify that many skincare products qualify as neither pure drugs nor pure cosmetics in the traditional sense, but a mix of the two, and call them ‘quasi-drugs.’ They permit cosmetics to contain pharmacologically active ingredients, provided that the medicinal effects are mild and the products'safety has been demonstrated. The legal jargon or lack thereof has left a lot of room for ambiguities and ad hoc interpretations resulting in skewed treatment of certain products by different regulatory authorities. The following example exemplifies the ambiguity; the following agents are regulated as drugs in the U.S., and as cosmetics in Europe: antiperspirants, anti-dandruff shampoos, and sunscreens.

retrieved_docs.title: Cosmeceuticals -- Introduction -- WORKING DEFINITION
retrieved_docs.content: For this activity, backed by a plethora of literature on the issue, a cosmeceutical may be characterized as: The product has pharmaceutical activity and is usable on normal or near-normal skin. The product should possess a defined benefit for minor skin disorders (cosmetic indication). The product possesses a very low-risk profile.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmaceutics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmaceutics is the overall process of developing a new chemical entity into an approved therapy that is safe and effective in treating or preventing disease. It is a complex process requiring multiple scientific, medical, legal, commercial, and regulatory expertise. On average, it typically takes at least ten years for a new drug to complete the process from initial discovery to the retail marketplace, with clinical trials alone taking six to seven years on average. The Tufts Center for the Study of Drug Development announced that it calculated that it costs pharmaceutical companies $2.6 billion to develop a new drug. [1] This figure has been subject to question, as the raw numbers on which they base the analysis have not been made available. The driving factor of this estimate is the cost of failed drug development attempts, with 80% of new compounds discontinued during their development. The steps involved in bringing a new chemical entity to the market as a drug involve the stages of discovery, chemical optimization and production, preclinical testing, pharmacology, and toxicology testing, early clinical trials establishing proof of concept, registration or pivotal clinical trials showing safety and efficacy in the target patient population and finally regulatory approval.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Macronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Lipids
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary fats can be separated into triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, sterols (cholesterol), and fatty acids. Fatty acids in the diet can be further distinguished according to the presence of double bonds; saturated fats have no double bonds, and unsaturated fats have one or more double bonds. [11] Finally, unsaturated fatty acids can be distinguished by the position of the first double bond counted from the methyl end of the carbon chain into omega-3, omega-6, and omega-9 fatty acids, with the first double bond occurring at the third, sixth, and ninth position, respectively. Different groups of dietary lipids have been known to have distinct physiological properties and health effects. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cosmeceuticals -- Introduction -- DEFINITIONS & CONTROVERSIES
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, cosmeceuticals are a segregated subclass within the domain of a cosmetic or drug. In Europe and Japan, cosmeceuticals are a subclass of cosmetics; however, in the US, cosmeceuticals can only be considered as a subclass of drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Lecithin Sphingomyelin Ratio -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The normal L/S ratio ranges from 2.0 to 2.5, indicating appropriate fetal lung development. An L/S ratio below 2.0 suggests fetal lung immaturity. For patients with poorly controlled diabetes, an L/S ratio of 3.0 was once considered due to concerns that elevated maternal glucose levels could delay fetal lung maturation. However, subsequent findings showed no significant difference in the L/S ratio between patients with and without diabetes. Some institutions continue to use 3.0 as the cutoff for pregnancies complicated by poorly controlled diabetes. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Pursed-lip Breathing -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Central and peripheral chemoreceptors govern the primary drive for respiration. Central chemoreceptors, located in the anterior medulla of the brainstem, predominantly respond to a decrease in pH caused by the accumulation of carbon dioxide in the cerebrospinal fluid. The blood-brain barrier protects the central nervous system from external stimuli. Still, the lipid-soluble nature of carbon dioxide allows it to diffuse across this barrier, rapidly influencing respiratory drive.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Macronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Lipids
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids or dietary fats are the most energy-dense macronutrient and provide 9 kcal of energy per gram. [7] In human physiology, lipids are essential for the production of sex hormones, maintenance of cellular structure, energy storage as body fat, regulation of body temperature, protection from physical trauma, and the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K. Additionally, fats enhance the taste, texture, and palatability of foods. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Bioavailability -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: For most purposes, bioavailability is defined as the fraction of the active form of a drug that reaches systemic circulation unaltered. This definition assumes 100% of the active drug that enters systemic circulation will successfully reach the target site. [4] However, one should understand that this definition excludes drugs that do not require access to systemic circulation for function (eg, certain topical drugs). The bioavailability of these drugs is measured by different parameters discussed elsewhere. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most LSDs have an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance. Three have an X-linked inheritance pattern: Hunter disease, Fabry disease, and Danon disease. Some mutations, however, do not produce a defective enzyme and are therefore not relevant to LSDs. Apart from genetics, inflammation and oxidative stress are crucial players in LSDs. [3] There are several classifications of LSDs. The commonest is according to the accumulated substrate. Many LSDs have the names of the persons who first described the conditions. The broad categories of LSDs, representative examples within the classes, and the accumulated substrates/defects are: I. Sphingolipidosis: Complex sphingosine containing phospholipids accumulate. All disorders comprise a mutant enzyme. Multiple sulfatase deficiency is the exception and is a post-translational disorder. A) GM2 gangliosidosis: Type A (Tay Sachs disease) Type O (Sandhoff disease) Type AB (GM2 activator deficiency)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: Nicotinic acid and its derivatives, identified as plain lipid‑modifying agents, are classified within the broader group of lipid‑modifying agents and are chemically members of the nicofuranose family. This places nicotinic acid‑based compounds both as therapeutic agents used to alter lipid profiles and as part of a specific structural class of nicofuranose compounds.

Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2603
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias) is classified as a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism. Within the broader group of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific type, and the category also includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose / new‑onset diabetes) as one of its members. Disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia), a condition that follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Additionally, Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën has a child concept, فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia), further linking it to the spectrum of lipid‑related disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate how various lipid metabolism disorders—including hyperlipoproteinemia, type IIa hyperlipidemia, and hyperlipidemia—are organized hierarchically, share common metabolic pathways, and may be associated with related conditions such as new‑onset diabetes, with specific genetic inheritance patterns informing patient risk assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a form of atherosclerotic plaque formation that occurs in the aorta abdominalis and is closely linked to lipid metabolism disorders, specifically the category “Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën,” which encompasses type II hyperlipidaemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني) and its subtype type IIa hyperlipidaemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. These dyslipidaemic states are metabolically associated with new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)), a condition that also exhibits altered urine output (تأثير إدرار البول). Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي), mapped to داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي, occupies a central role in cardiovascular pathology as a child concept of chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد) and a parent concept for the broader domain of Blood, Heart and Circulation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1341
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑D‑glucopyranose is classified as a saccharide, belonging to the broad group of carbohydrate molecules known as saccharidy. As a specific sugar monomer, beta‑D‑glucopyranose exemplifies the structural and functional characteristics of saccharides, which serve as essential energy sources, metabolic intermediates, and structural components in biological systems. Recognizing beta‑D‑glucopyranose as a member of the saccharidy class aids in understanding its role in nutrition, biochemical pathways, and laboratory analyses of carbohydrate content.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic diseases that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. In addition to lipid abnormalities, this category is linked to elevated blood glucose conditions such as new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)), highlighting the close interplay between lipid and glucose metabolic pathways. Recognizing that hyperlipoproteinemia and early diabetes can coexist within the spectrum of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores the importance of integrated assessment and management of both lipid and glucose abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën, a broad classification of metabolic disorders affecting lipoproteins and other lipid abnormalities, includes the specific condition فرط ليبيدات الدم, which denotes hyperlipidemia characterized by elevated blood lipid levels. Recognizing فرط ليبيدات الدم as a child entity within this larger category helps clinicians organize lipid disorders and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 679
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias) is classified as a child of the broader category disorders of lipid metabolism. Within disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a related subclass, and the specific condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to this category. Autosomal dominant inheritance is identified as the inheritance type of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, indicating a genetic basis for this form of hyperlipidemia. Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën also has the child concept فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia), further linking various lipid‑related disorders under a common framework. In addition, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (hyperglycemia/early diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, illustrating the clinical overlap between glucose dysregulation and lipid abnormalities. This hyperglycemic condition is a member of التجفاف (dehydration) and is associated with تأثير إدرار البول (increased urine output), reflecting its typical physiological effects. Together, these relationships demonstrate that lipid metabolism disorders encompass a spectrum of conditions—including hyperlipoproteinemia, type IIa hyperlipidemia, and broader hyperlipidemia—some of which follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, and that they are interrelated with glucose metabolism disturbances such as hyperglycemia, which can lead to dehydration and diuresis. Recognizing these interconnected pathways is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: these conditions. Disorders of lipid metabolism form a distinct clinical group that includes lipoprotein and other lipid metabolism disorders (Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën) and manifestations such as xanthoma due to primary hypercholesterolemia. This group is classified as a fat‑metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الدهون) and is inversely related to the broader metabolic disorder concept (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي). Within this group, hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and is a child of the lipid‑metabolism disorder node. Hyperlipidemia (فرط ليبيدات الدم) is a child entity of the same lipid‑metabolism category. The lipid‑metabolism cluster also contains new‑onset diabetes mellitus (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد), whose member effect of increased urination (تأثير إدرار البول) reflects the classic polyuric symptom of early diabetes, illustrating the close link
